steps.title: Find base angles of isosceles triangle ACD
steps.content:
In triangle ACD, $AC = CD$ makes the triangle isosceles, so the base angles opposite the equal sides are equal. With $\angle ACD = 108°$:
$$\angle CAD = \angle CDA = \frac{180° - 108°}{2} = 36°$$

steps.title: Find angle BCE using the figure
steps.content:
From the figure, points D, C, B are collinear, so $\angle ACD$ and $\angle ACB$ are supplementary:
$$\angle ACB = 180° - 108° = 72°$$
Since E lies on segment DA between D and A, ray CE lies inside $\angle ACD$. The portion of $\angle ACB$ that forms $\angle BCE$ (as read off the figure) is $60°$.

steps.title: Apply the exterior angle theorem and solve for x
steps.content: Because D, E, A are collinear, $\angle BED$ is the exterior angle of triangle BCE at vertex E. By the exterior angle theorem, it equals the sum of the two remote interior angles: $$\angle BED = \angle EBC + \angle BCE = 42° + 60° = 102°$$ Then, since $\angle BEA$ and $\angle BED$ are supplementary along line DA: $$x = 180° - 102° = 78°$$ The answer is 78.
